text: The surgeon marks the borders of liver segment IVb before performing an en bloc cholecystectomy. A Pringle maneuver is applied using the Hohen Loop technique prior to the resection of segment IVb. After the resection, the Pringle is removed, and hemostasis is achieved using bipolar cautery and hemostatic agents.

The surgeon performs a robotic right hemihepatectomy, beginning with the dissection of the liver hilum, which shows tense adhesions from previous surgery. The right hepatic duct is encircled, clipped, and transected, followed by the right hepatic artery. The embolized right portal vein is dissected amidst visible inflammation, then encircled, clipped, and transected.

The surgeon mobilizes the liver by transecting the falciform ligament and detaching it from the vena cava, clipping and removing short hepatic veins. Intraoperative ultrasound is used to verify the line of transection before performing parenchymal transection. TachoSeal is applied for hemostasis, and the falciform ligament is repaired. A silicone drain is placed at the end of the procedure.

Here, the perioperative data for stage I and II are shown. Two-stage robotic liver surgery is a valid option for colorectal metastasis. It offers a chance of cure and it can be performed safely in the hands of an experienced surgeon.
